WMT.quantitative_report:
# WMT Quantitative Report — Decision Date 2024-07-01

---

## 1. Trajectory: Revenue, Earnings, Margins, Cash Flow, Balance Sheet

**Revenue:** TTM revenue stands at $657.33B. Latest Q1 (2024-04-30) revenue is $161.51B, up +6.0% YoY from $152.30B (Q1 2023-04-30) but down −6.9% QoQ from $173.39B (Q4 2024-01-31). Revenue shows a step-up pattern: quarterly figures ranged $152.30B–$152.86B in mid-2022, then $160.80B–$173.39B through 2023–2024.

**Earnings:** TTM net income is $19.68B. Latest Q1 net income is $5.31B vs. $1.90B a year prior (+179.9% YoY). EPS diluted is $0.63 vs. $0.62 YoY (+1.6%), reflecting a 3-for-1 stock split (effective ~Feb 2024). Quarterly EPS is volatile: $2.92 (Q2 2023), $0.17 (Q3 2023), −$1.80 (Q4 2024 despite $5.68B reported net income — data anomaly flagged), $0.63 (Q1 2024).

**Margins (latest Q1 YoY):** Gross margin improved to 24.8% ($40.08B/$161.51B) from 24.3% ($37.02B/$152.30B). Operating margin at 4.2% ($6.84B/$161.51B) vs. 4.1% ($6.24B/$152.30B). Net margin at 3.3% vs. 1.2%. TTM gross margin: 24.5% ($161.04B/$657.33B); TTM operating margin: 4.2% ($27.61B/$657.33B); TTM net margin: 3.0% ($19.68B/$657.33B).

**Cash Flow:** All quarterly cash flow data is unavailable. Free cash flow is marked n/a throughout. Cash generation cannot be assessed.

**Balance Sheet:** Total assets grew from $243.20B (Jan 2023) to $252.40B (Jan 2024) to $254.05B (Apr 2024). Total equity was $83.75B (Jan 2023), $90.35B (Jan 2024), $88.07B (Apr 2024). Total debt reported at $39.58B (Jan 2024) vs. $38.84B (Jan 2023). Debt-to-equity: 0.44x ($39.58B / $90.35B).

---

## 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings Quality — Concerns:**
- Extreme quarterly EPS volatility: range of −$1.80 to +$2.92 over eight quarters. The $2.92 Q2-2023 and −$1.80 Q4-2024 bracket much tighter operating income figures ($7.32B and $7.25B respectively), suggesting non-operating items dominate EPS swings.
- Data anomaly: Q4 (2024-01-31) reports net income of $5.68B but EPS diluted of −$1.80. This inconsistency cannot be reconciled from supplied data and casts uncertainty on reported earnings normalization.
- TTM net margin of 3.0% is thin, leaving minimal buffer against cost shocks.

**Balance-Sheet Resilience — Adequate:**
- Leverage is conservative: 0.44x debt-to-equity. Total debt of $39.58B is modest relative to total assets of $252.40B (15.7% asset ratio).
- Equity base of $88.07B–$90.35B is stable.
- ROE of 22.3% (latest) reflects reasonable capital efficiency, though partly driven by leverage.
- **Limitation:** No cash or current-asset/liability breakdowns are available, so liquidity and interest coverage cannot be verified.

---

## 3. Valuation vs. Own History

| Metric | Latest (2024-06-28) | 12-Month Range | 12-Month Low |
|---|---|---|---|
| PE diluted | **27.812** | 8.3 – 35.2 | 8.315 (Dec 2023) |
| Price/Book | **6.215** | 1.6 – 6.3 | 1.593 (Dec 2023) |
| Earnings yield | **3.6%** | — | — |
| Net margin | 3.0% | 0.0 – 0.0* | — |
| ROE | 22.3% | 0.1 – 0.2* | — |

*Range data shows compressed values likely due to rounding in source.

**Key observation:** The PE has expanded **3.3x** from its trough of 8.315 (Dec 2023) to 27.812 (Jun 2024). P/B has expanded **3.9x** from 1.593 to 6.215 over the same span. The current PE sits at the **79th percentile** of its 12-month range (27.8 / 35.2 = 0.79 toward the max). P/B is at essentially the **98th percentile** (6.215 / 6.3). This is a near-complete re-rating to the top of the historical window.

Net margin did improve from ~1.8% to 3.0% and ROE from ~14% to 22.3%, providing some fundamental justification. However, the multiple expansion far exceeds the margin improvement in magnitude.

---

## 4. Price Level & Returns vs. Fundamentals

| Period | Price | Delta |
|---|---|---|
| 2024-06-28 | **$67.71** | — |
| 2024-03-28 | $60.17 | **+12.5%** |
| 2023-12-27 | $52.63 | +14.3% |
| 2023-09-27 | $53.91 | −2.4% |
| 2023-06-28 | $51.78 | +4.1% |
| **Annual return** | | **+30.8%** |
| 52-week range | | $49.85 – $69.04 |

The stock is within **1.9% of its 52-week high** ($69.04). The +30.8% annual return substantially exceeds the +6.0% YoY revenue growth and even the +179.9% net income growth (off a depressed base). The disconnect is dominated by **multiple expansion**, not proportional fundamental improvement. The last two quarterly returns of +14.3% and +12.5% represent accelerating momentum that has outpaced the flat-to-mixed underlying EPS trajectory.

---

## 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

**Upside:**
- Sustained margin expansion: if net margin can hold at 3.0% (vs. 1.8% a year ago) on a $657B+ revenue base, TTM earnings power is $19.7B, supporting the higher valuation.
- Revenue trajectory has stepped up durably ($152B–$153B quarters in 2022 → $160B–$173B in 2023–2024), suggesting structural growth.
- Conservative balance sheet (0.44x D/E) provides financial flexibility.

**Downside:**
- **Valuation mean-reversion risk is the dominant quantitative threat.** A reversion to the 12-month median PE (~10–12x) from the current 27.8x, holding earnings constant at TTM $19.68B (~$2.45 diluted post-split), would imply a price of ~$24–$29, representing **57–65% downside** from $67.71. Even a partial reversion to 18–20x implies ~$44–$49 (−28% to −35%).
- **No cash flow verification:** Free cash flow is entirely absent from the data. If operating cash flow conversion is poor (plausible given asset-heavy retail), the earnings quality discount should be larger.
- Quarterly earnings volatility (EPS range −$1.80 to +$2.92) makes forward estimates unreliable, increasing the probability of an earnings miss that could trigger multiple compression.
- Price at 52-week highs with two consecutive double-digit quarterly gains creates elevated mean-reversion probability.

---

## 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

The fundamental trajectory shows genuine improvement: +6% YoY revenue growth, margin expansion (gross +50bp, net nearly doubled), and a conservatively leveraged balance sheet. However, the **valuation has moved to near-peak multiples** within the supplied 12-month window (PE 27.8x at the 79th percentile; P/B 6.2x at the 98th percentile). This re-rating has absorbed a substantial portion of the fundamental improvement.

The risk/reward profile is **asymmetric to the downside** at current levels. The spread between the current PE (27.8x) and the historical median (~10–12x) implies that even modest earnings disappointment could trigger outsized price declines through multiple compression. The +30.8% annual return has significantly outpaced the +6% revenue growth trajectory, with the gap filled almost entirely by multiple expansion rather than demonstrated earnings durability. Absent cash flow data to independently verify earnings quality, and with confirmed quarterly EPS volatility (including a data-anomalous negative quarter), the quantitative case for further upside at $67.71 / 27.8x PE requires sustained margin gains that are not yet established across a full cycle. **Quantitative evidence points to unfavorable risk/reward at the current valuation juncture.**
WMT.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Analysis Report: Walmart (WMT)**
**Decision Date: 2024-07-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read**
Based on the 2023 10-K (period end 2023-01-31), Walmart is a global omni-channel retailer operating under three segments: Walmart U.S. (69% of FY2023 net sales), Walmart International (17%), and Sam's Club (14%). The franchise is built on a foundation of Everyday Low Prices (EDLP) and Everyday Low Costs (EDLC), serving ~240 million customers weekly across over 10,500 stores and eCommerce sites in 20 countries. The strategy focuses on being a "people-led, technology-powered" omni-channel retailer, aiming to create a "flywheel effect" through integrated stores, eCommerce, and expanding services (e.g., Walmart Connect advertising, Walmart Fulfillment Services, health & wellness, financial services like PhonePe in India). Walmart U.S. drives the majority of sales and profit, while Walmart International involves strategic portfolio reshaping for local relevance.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated)**
*   **Positive Financial Execution (2024-05-16):** Walmart raised its full-year fiscal 2025 forecast after reporting better-than-expected Q1 results. The updated guidance projected reaches at the high-end or slightly above its original outlook for net sales growth of 3%-4% and operating income growth of 4%-6%. This was driven by performance betting on easing inflation to boost sales of groceries and non-essential merchandise.
*   **Stock Performance (2024-06-27):** On this date, Walmart's stock declined 0.6% despite broader market gains, indicating potential valuation sensitivities or sector rotation.
*   **Valuation Context (2024-05-14):** Pre-earnings, analysts noted Walmart's valuation had risen to about 25 times expected earnings, up from its 10-year average of ~20 times, signaling elevated market expectations for profit growth.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Legal and Regulatory Risk:** The company faces noted legal and regulatory exposures. These include **(2024-05-21)** shareholder concerns and risk notifications regarding racial discrimination, bias, and disparities that could lead to legal actions, regulatory sanctions, and reputational damage. **(2024-05-30)** Historical allegations of predatory pricing were cited. **(2024-06-18)** A state Attorney General announced a $1.64 million settlement with Walmart to resolve pricing violation and unconscionable business practice allegations under state consumer protection laws. **(2024-06-28)** General commentary on potential wrongful termination lawsuits was present.
*   **Competitive Intensity:** The 10-K explicitly describes highly competitive and rapidly evolving retail and omni-channel landscapes across all segments, with risks from new business models and well-funded competitors (e.g., Amazon, Costco). **(Offset from 10-K)**: Walmart's scale, EDLP value proposition, supply chain automation, and expanding service ecosystem (advertising, fulfillment, healthcare) are cited as competitive advantages.
*   **International Portfolio Complexity:** The strategy of reshaping the Walmart International portfolio for long-term profitability (referenced in 10-K as divESTitures in Argentina, UK, Japan, some African ops) introduces execution and cultural risks. **(Offset)**: This is framed as a deliberate strategic action to strengthen the portfolio.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral-to-Positive.** The recent narrative is dominated by strong operational execution and a raised positive outlook for fiscal 2025 **(2024-05-16)**, reflecting the resilience of its value-oriented model. This is partially offset by a cluster of legal, regulatory, and reputational risks, some historical and some current **(2024-05-21, 2024-06-18)**, which are typical for a retailer of its scale and market power but require monitoring. The franchise's core strengths in omni-channel integration and scale remain intact, but the elevated valuation **(2024-05-14)** and intense competition frame the near-term opportunity. The balance of evidence points to a stable, competitively positioned business demonstrating momentum, complicated by endemic legal and regulatory scrutiny.

*Evidence Confirmed From: 10-K filing (2023-03-17), Reuters/Yahoo/The Motley Fool/NJ OAG articles dated 2024-05-14 through 2024-06-28 as listed.*
*Inference: None; all qualitative assessments are drawn from the supplied narrative text.*

corrections.issue: The quant report states 'PE has expanded 3.3x from its trough of 8.315 (Dec 2023) to 27.812 (Jun 2024)' — this is a 3.3x multiple, but the raw data shows the PE jumped sharply between Feb 2024 (9.359) and Mar 2024 (32.173), likely due to the stock split and/or earnings timing, not a gradual re-rating. The report does not flag this structural break, which could mislead the PM into thinking the expansion is purely sentiment-driven rather than partly mechanical (post-split EPS denominator change).
corrections.required_action: Note that the PE jump from ~9x to ~32x between Feb and Mar 2024 likely reflects the 3-for-1 stock split effect on diluted share count and/or TTM earnings denominator reset, not solely market re-rating. The '3.3x expansion' framing overstates the market-driven component.

corrections.issue: The quant report's downside scenario calculates 'a reversion to the 12-month median PE (~10–12x)' from constant at TTM $19.68B (~$2.45 diluted post-split), would imply a price of ~$24–$29, representing 57–65% downside.' However, the raw data shows the 12-month median PE is heavily skewed by the pre-split period. The post-split PE has ranged 27.8–34.2x (Mar–Jun 2024), so using 10–12x as the 'median' conflates pre- and post-split regimes and produces an unrealistically bearish target.
corrections.required_action: The 10–12x median PE is a pre-split artifact. Post-split PE has consistently been 27–34x. The downside scenario should use the post-split range (~27–34x) or explicitly acknowledge the structural break, rather than implying 57–65% downside from a mechanically distorted historical median.

corrections.issue: The qual report cites 'elevated valuation (2024-05-14)' referencing 'about 25 times expected earnings, up from its 10-year average of ~20 times.' The raw evidence confirms this from the Yahoo article. However, the quant report's latest PE is 27.8x (post-split), while the qual report's 25x is pre-split or forward-looking. These are not directly comparable figures, and neither report flags the discrepancy.
corrections.required_action: Acknowledge that the qual report's '25x expected earnings' (forward PE, likely pre-split basis) and the quant report's '27.8x diluted PE' (trailing, post-split) measure different things and are not directly comparable without adjustment.

unsupported_claims: The quant report states 'ROE of 22.3% (latest) reflects reasonable capital efficiency' — the raw data confirms ROE=0.223 for 2024-06-28, but the report does not note that ROE jumped from 0.18 (Apr 2024) to 0.223 (Jun 2024) in just two months with no corresponding balance sheet update, suggesting the ROE figure may be stale or based on mismatched period-end data.

The quant report claims 'The PE has expanded 3.3x from its trough of 8.315 (Dec 2023) to 27.812 (Jun 2024)' and characterizes this as market re-rating, but the raw data shows the PE jumped from 9.359 (Feb 2024) to 32.173 (Mar 2024) — a 3.4x jump in one month coinciding with the stock split period. The report does not acknowledge this structural break as a potential data artifact.

The quant report states 'A reversion to the 12-month median PE (~10–12x)' — the raw sparse history shows 12 data points, 8 of which are pre-split (Jul 2023–Feb 2024, range 8.3–12.9x) and 4 post-split (Mar–Jun 2024, range 27.8–34.2x). The 'median' of ~10–12x is driven entirely by the pre-split regime and is not a valid comparator for the current post-split PE.

- The quant report states 'A reversion to the 12-month median PE (~10–12x)' — the raw sparse history shows 12 data points, 8 of which are pre-split (Jul 2023–Feb 2024, range 8.3–12.9x) and 4 post-split (Mar–Jun 2024, range 27.8–34.2x). The 'median' of ~10–12x is driven entirely by the pre-split regime and is not a valid comparator for the current post-split PE.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Walmart's fundamental momentum is genuine—Q1 FY2025 beat, raised guidance, and margin expansion—but the stock has already re-rated to near-peak multiples (trailing PE ~28x post-split, P/B 6.2x at 98th percentile of range), absorbing most of the upside. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels, with limited further re-rating room unless margins sustainably expand beyond current levels.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Walmart's fundamental momentum is genuine—Q1 FY2025 beat, raised guidance, and margin expansion—but the stock has already re-rated to near-peak multiples (trailing PE ~28x post-split, P/B 6.2x at 98th percentile of range), absorbing most of the upside. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels, with limited further re-rating room unless margins sustainably expand beyond current levels.
key_factors: Q1 FY2025 beat and raised guidance (2024-05-16): net sales growth guidance of 3-4% and operating income growth of 4-6% at high-end or above, with TTM revenue of $657.3B and net income of $19.7B demonstrating operational momentum.

Valuation near peak: trailing PE of 27.8x (post-split) sits at 79th percentile of 12-month range; P/B of 6.2x at 98th percentile. The +30.8% annual return has outpaced +6% YoY revenue growth, with the gap filled by multiple expansion rather than proportional earnings improvement.

Margin expansion provides fundamental support: gross margin improved to 24.8% from 24.3% YoY, net margin nearly doubled to 3.0% from 1.2%, and ROE at 22.3% on a conservatively leveraged balance sheet (0.44x D/E).

Legal and regulatory headwinds persist: multiple recent settlements and allegations (2024-05-21, 2024-05-30, 2024-06-18) create reputational and financial risk, though these are typical for a retailer of Walmart's scale.

Item 1. Business.
Overview
JPMorgan Chase & Co. ("JPMorgan Chase" or the "Firm", NYSE: JPM), a financial holding company incorporated under Delaware law in 1968, is a leading financial services firm based in the United States of America ("U.S."), with operations worldwide. JPMorgan Chase had $3.7 trillion in assets and $292.3 billion in stockholders' equity as of December 31, 2022. The Firm is a leader in investment banking, financial services for consumers and small businesses, commercial banking, financial transaction processing and asset management. Under the J.P. Morgan and Chase brands, the Firm serves millions of customers, predominantly in the U.S., and many of the world's most prominent corporate, institutional and government clients globally.
JPMorgan Chase's principal bank subsidiary is JPMorgan Chase Bank, National Association ("JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A."), a national banking association with U.S. branches in 48 states and Washington, D.C. JPMorgan Chase's principal non-bank subsidiary is J.P. Morgan Securities LLC ("J.P. Morgan Securities"), a U.S. broker-dealer. The bank and non-bank subsidiaries of JPMorgan Chase operate nationally as well as through overseas branches and subsidiaries, representative offices and subsidiary foreign banks. The Firm's principal operating subsidiaries outside the U.S. are J.P. Morgan Securities plc and J.P. Morgan SE ("JPMSE"), which are subsidiaries of JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. and are based in the United Kingdom ("U.K.") and Germany, respectively.
The Firm's website is www.jpmorganchase.com. JPMorgan Chase makes available on its website, free of charge, annual reports on Form 10-K, quarterly reports on Form 10-Q and current reports on Form 8-K pursuant to Section 13(a) or Section 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as soon as reasonably practicable after it electronically files or furnishes such material to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC") at www.sec.gov. JPMorgan Chase makes new and important information about the Firm available on its website at https://www.jpmorganchase.com, including on the Investor Relations section of its website at https://www.jpmorganchase.com/ir. Information on the Firm's website is not incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2022 ("2022 Form 10-K" or "Form 10-K") or the Firm's other filings with the SEC. The Firm has adopted, and posted on its website, a Code of Conduct for all employees of the Firm and a Code of Ethics for its Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, Chief Financial Officer, Principal Accounting Officer and all other professionals of the Firm worldwide serving in a finance, accounting, treasury, tax or investor relations role. The Code of Ethics is also available in print upon request to the Firm's Investor Relations team. Within the time period required by the SEC, JPMorgan Chase will post on its website any amendment to the Code of Ethics and any waiver applicable to a director or executive officer.
Business segments
For management reporting purposes, JPMorgan Chase's activities are organized into four major reportable business segments, as well as a Corporate segment. The Firm's consumer business is the Consumer & Community Banking ("CCB") segment. The Firm's wholesale business segments are the Corporate & Investment Bank ("CIB"), Commercial Banking ("CB"), and Asset & Wealth Management ("AWM").
A description of the Firm's business segments and the products and services they provide to their respective client bases is provided in the "Business segment results" section of Management's discussion and analysis of financial condition and results of operations ("Management's discussion and analysis" or "MD&A"), beginning on page 46 and in Note 32.
Competition
JPMorgan Chase and its subsidiaries and affiliates operate in highly competitive environments. Competitors include other banks, brokerage firms, investment banking companies, merchant banks, hedge funds, commodity trading companies, private equity firms, insurance companies, mutual fund companies, investment managers, credit card companies, mortgage banking companies, trust companies, securities processing companies, automobile financing companies, leasing companies, e-commerce and other internet-based companies, financial technology companies, and other companies engaged in providing similar as well as new products and services. The Firm's businesses generally compete on the basis of the quality and variety of the Firm's products and services, transaction execution, innovation, reputation and price. Competition also varies based on the types of clients, customers, industries and geographies served. With respect to some of its geographies and products, JPMorgan Chase competes globally; with respect to others, the Firm competes on a national or regional basis. New competitors in the financial services industry continue to emerge, including firms that offer products and services solely through the internet and non-financial companies that offer products and services that disintermediate traditional banking products and services offered by financial services firms such as JPMorgan Chase.

JPM.quantitative_report:
**JPM Quantitative Assessment (Data as of 2024-07-01)**

**1. Financial Trajectory**
- **Revenue:** Grew from $30.71B (Q2 2022) to $41.93B (Q1 2024). Latest YoY revenue growth is +9.3% (from $38.35B).
- **Earnings:** EPS diluted increased from $2.76 (Q2 2022) to $4.44 (Q1 2024). Latest YoY EPS growth is +8.3% (from $4.10). QoQ growth is +45.6% (from $3.05).
- **Margins:** Latest net margin is 31.1%, operating margin is 38.9%. Net margin has ranged between 30.8% and 33.3% over the past year.
- **Cash Flow:** No cash flow data available.
- **Balance Sheet:** Total assets grew from $3841.31B (Q2 2022) to $4090.73B (Q1 2024). Total equity increased from $286.14B to $336.64B. Equity/Assets ratio improved from 7.45% to 8.23%. Leverage (Liabilities/Equity) decreased from 12.42x to 11.15x.

**2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience**
- **Earnings Quality:** Consistent net margins (~31%) and ROE (15.0%) suggest stable operating profitability. No cash flow data limits assessment of accruals and cash conversion.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** Equity capital has grown steadily, improving capital adequacy. Leverage remains high but is improving. Total equity increased by 17.6% over the eight-quarter period.

**3. Valuation vs. Own History**
- Latest valuation: PE diluted=11.701, P/B=1.75, P/S=3.644, earnings yield=8.5%.
- Over the past year, PE diluted ranged from 7.9 to 12.3, and P/B from 1.3 to 1.8.
- Current multiples are near the top of their historical ranges. PE has expanded from 8.255 (Nov 2023) to 11.701, and P/B from 1.333 to 1.75.

**4. Price Context vs. Fundamentals**
- Latest price: $202.26 (2024-06-28). 52-week range: $135.19 to $205.88.
- Annual return: +45.9%. Quarterly return: +1.0% (from $200.30).
- Price appreciation has outpaced fundamental growth: price +45.9% YoY vs. EPS +8.3% YoY, indicating multiple expansion as the primary driver.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside**
- **Upside:** If TTM EPS of ~$16.67 (calculated from TTM net income $50.35B and ~3.02B shares) is sustained and PE expands to the top of its range (12.3), price could reach ~$205.04, approximating the 52-week high.
- **Downside:** If PE contracts to the lower end of its range (7.9) or P/B to 1.3, price could fall to ~$131.69 (using PE) or ~$144.91 (using P/B), based on current earnings and book value per share of ~$111.47.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
- Fundamentals show growth, but valuation is stretched relative to its history. Price has significantly outpaced earnings growth over the past year, relying on multiple expansion. Risk/reward appears balanced to cautious, with limited near-term valuation support and potential for mean reversion in multiples if earnings momentum slows.
JPM.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM)**
**Decision Date: 2024-07-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read**
Based on the 2022 10-K (filing date 2023-02-21), JPMorgan Chase is a leading, globally diversified financial services firm. Its operations are organized into four main segments: Consumer & Community Banking (CCB), Corporate & Investment Bank (CIB), Commercial Banking (CB), and Asset & Wealth Management (AWM). The firm competes across a broad spectrum of financial services against banks, fintechs, and other non-traditional competitors, emphasizing product quality, innovation, and reputation.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated)**
*   **Capital & Regulation (2024-06-27):** All 31 banks, including JPMorgan, passed the Federal Reserve's 2024 stress test. However, a Goldman Sachs analyst characterized the results as "worse than expected" and "disappointing," suggesting potential market concern over the severity of modeled losses.
*   **Growth Outlook (2024-05-20):** JPMorgan estimated its second-quarter investment banking revenue would grow by a percentage in the mid-teens, citing improved market conditions. Markets revenue was expected to climb by a mid-single-digit percentage.
*   **Strategic Expansion (2024-05-30):** The bank plans to grow its headcount in India by 5%-7% annually over the next few years.
*   **Geographic Outlook (2024-06-21):** JPMorgan's asset and wealth management CEO reported seeing signs of an improving economy in China, which is expected to bolster business there.
*   **Legal Settlements (2024-06-28):** JPMorgan, along with other major banks, agreed to pay a total of $46 million to settle an interest-rate swap case.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risks:**
    *   **Regulatory & Legal:** The bank faces multiple, concurrent regulatory actions. It paid a $100 million fine to the CFTC (2024-05-29) and expects an additional $100 million penalty for trade surveillance failures (2024-06-03). It is also facing a potential fine for internal control deficiencies (2024-06-13). These indicate persistent operational and compliance challenges.
    *   **Geopolitical:** JPMorgan is preparing for the potential seizure of its assets in Russia due to lawsuits (2024-05-12).
    *   **Competition:** The 10-K highlights intense competition from traditional and new entrants (fintechs, internet-based firms), which could pressure margins and market share.
*   **Offsets:**
    *   **Franchise Strength & Diversification:** The firm's scale, global reach, and leadership across multiple business segments provide resilience.
    *   **Growth Initiatives:** Active investment in key areas like investment banking and strategic geographic expansion (India, China) positions the firm for future growth.
    *   **Capital Resilience:** Passing the 2024 stress test, despite the market's negative reaction to the results, confirms the firm meets regulatory capital requirements under severe stress.

**4. Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral.** JPMorgan Chase maintains a powerful, diversified financial franchise with clear strategic growth initiatives. However, the near-term narrative is dominated by a series of significant regulatory fines and legal settlements, indicating material operational and compliance risks. The market's negative reaction to the stress test results, despite a pass, adds a layer of uncertainty regarding capital return expectations. The balance between the firm's strong competitive position and its ongoing regulatory headwinds results in a neutral qualitative outlook.

However, the share count of ~3.02B is not explicitly stated in the raw evidence. The implied share count can be back-calculated: $50.35B / $16.67 ≈ 3.02B shares. pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: JPM's franchise quality and earnings momentum remain best-in-class, but the stock is now priced near the top of its historical valuation range (P/E 11.7, P/B 1.75) after a +45.9% annual run, while Q1 2024 earnings showed a strong +45.6% QoQ rebound from Q4's trough. The risk/reward is balanced, with limited upside from current multiples and ongoing regulatory headwinds offsetting solid fundamentals.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: JPM's franchise quality and earnings momentum remain best-in-class, but the stock is now priced near the top of its historical valuation range (P/E 11.7, P/B 1.75) after a +45.9% annual run, while Q1 2024 earnings showed a strong +45.6% QoQ rebound from Q4's trough. The risk/reward is balanced, with limited upside from current multiples and ongoing regulatory headwinds offsetting solid fundamentals.
key_factors: Q1 2024 EPS diluted of $4.44 rebounded +45.6% QoQ from Q4 2023's $3.05, with YoY growth of +8.3%, confirming earnings recovery after the prior quarter's -29.6% QoQ decline.

Valuation is stretched: P/E of 11.701 and P/B of 1.75 sit near the top of their 365-day ranges (7.9-12.3 and 1.3-1.8 respectively), with price +45.9% YoY far outpacing EPS +8.3% YoY, indicating multiple expansion as the primary driver.

Regulatory headwinds persist with a $100M CFTC fine (2024-05-29), an expected $100M penalty for trade surveillance failures (2024-06-03), and a potential fine for internal control deficiencies (2024-06-13), though the bank passed the 2024 stress test (2024-06-27).

Balance sheet resilience improved with equity/assets ratio rising from 7.45% to 8.23% and leverage declining from 12.42x to 11.15x over eight quarters, while net margins remain stable at ~31% and ROE at ~15%.

Item 1. Business
The Boeing Company, together with its subsidiaries (herein referred to as "Boeing," the "Company," "we," "us," "our"), is one of the world's major aerospace firms.
We are organized based on the products and services we offer. We operate in four reportable segments:
- 
Commercial Airplanes (BCA);
- 
Defense, Space & Security (BDS);
- 
Global Services (BGS);
- 
Boeing Capital (BCC).
Commercial Airplanes Segment
This segment develops, produces and markets commercial jet aircraft principally to the commercial airline industry worldwide. We are a leading producer of commercial aircraft and offer a family of commercial jetliners designed to meet a broad spectrum of global passenger and cargo requirements of airlines. This family of commercial jet aircraft in production includes the 737 narrow-body model and the 767, 777 and 787 wide-body models. We ended production of the 747 wide-body model in 2022. Development continues on the 777X program and the 737-7 and 737-10 derivatives.
Defense, Space & Security Segment
This segment engages in the research, development, production and modification of manned and unmanned military aircraft and weapons systems for strike, surveillance and mobility, including fighter and trainer aircraft; vertical lift, including rotorcraft and tilt-rotor aircraft; and commercial derivative aircraft, including anti-submarine and tanker aircraft. In addition, this segment engages in the research, development, production and modification of the following products and related services: strategic defense and intelligence systems, including strategic missile and defense systems, command, control, communications, computers, intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance (C4ISR),
…[truncated 8603 chars]…
ion (FAA) and the Department of Homeland Security. Similar government authorities exist in our non-U.S. markets.
Government Contracts.
The U.S. government, and other governments, may terminate any of our government contracts at their convenience, as well as for default based on our failure to meet specified performance requirements. If any of our U.S. government contracts were to be terminated for convenience, we generally would be entitled to receive payment for work completed and allowable termination or cancellation costs. If any of our government contracts were to be terminated for default, generally the U.S. government would pay only for the work that has been accepted and could require us to pay the difference between the original contract price and the cost to re-procure the contract items, net of the work accepted from the original contract. The U.S. government can also hold us liable for damages resulting from the default.
Commercial Aircraft.
In the U.S., our commercial aircraft products are required to comply with FAA regulations governing production and quality systems, airworthiness and installation approvals, repair procedures and continuing operational safety. New aircraft models and new derivative aircraft are required to obtain FAA certification prior to entry into service. Outside the U.S., similar requirements exist for airworthiness, installation and operational approvals. These requirements are generally administered by the national aviation authorities of each country and, in the case of Europe, coordinated by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency.
Environmental.
We are subject to various federal, state, local and non-U.S. laws and regulations relating to environmental protection, including the discharge, treatment, storage, disposal and remediation of hazardous substances and wastes. We could also be affected by laws and regulations relating to climate change, including laws limiting or otherwise related to greenhouse gas emissions. These laws and regulations could lead to increased environmental compliance expenditures, increased energy and raw materials costs and new and/or additional investment in designs and technologies. We continually assess our compliance status and management of environmental matters to ensure our operations are in compliance with all applicable environmental laws and regulations. Investigation, remediation and operation and maintenance costs associated with environmental compliance and management of sites are a normal, recurring part of our operations. These costs often are allowable costs under our contracts with the U.S. government. It is reasonably possible that costs incurred to ensure continued environmental compliance could have a material impact on our results of operations, financial condition or cash flows if additional work requirements or more stringent clean-up standards are imposed by regulators, new areas of soil, air and groundwater contamination are discovered and/or expansions of work scope are prompted by the results of investigations.
For additional information relating to environmental contingencies, see Note 13 to our Consolidated Financial Statements.
Non-U.S. Sales.
Our non-U.S. sales are subject to both U.S. and non-U.S. governmental regulations and procurement policies and practices, including regulations relating to import-export control, tariffs, investment, exchange controls, anti-corruption and repatriation of earnings. Non-U.S. sales are also subject to varying currency, political and economic risks.
Raw Materials, Parts and Subassemblies
We are highly dependent on the availability of essential materials, parts and subassemblies from our suppliers and subcontractors. The most important raw materials required for our aerospace products
4
Table of Contents
are aluminum (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions), titanium (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions) and composites (including carbon and boron). Although alternative sources generally exist for these raw materials, qualification of the sources could take a year or more. During 2022, as a result of the Russia Ukraine war, we suspended purchasing titanium from Russia. This has not disrupted our operations as we have been able to use inventory on hand and identify alternative sources. Many major components and product equipment items are procured or subcontracted on a sole-source basis. We continue to work with a small number of sole-source suppliers to ensure continuity of supply for certain items.
Suppliers
We are dependent upon the ability of a large number of U.S. and non-U.S. suppliers and subcontractors to meet performance specifications, quality standards and delivery schedules at our anticipated costs. While we maintain an extensive qualification and performance surveillance system to control risk associated with such reliance on third parties, failure of suppliers or subcontractors to meet commitments could adversely affect production schedules and program/contract profitability, thereby jeopardizing our ability to fulfill commitments to our customers. We are also dependent on the availability of energy sources, such as electricity, at affordable prices.
Seasonality
No material portion of our business is considered to be seasonal.
Executive Officers of the Registrant
See "Item 10. Directors, Executive Officers and Corporate Governance" in Part III.
Other Information
Boeing was originally incorporated in the State of Washington in 1916 and reincorporated in Delaware in 1934. Our principal executive offices are located at 929 Long Bridge Drive, Arlington, Virginia 22202, and our telephone number is (703) 465-3500.
General information about us can be found at www.boeing.com. The information contained on or connected to our website is not incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K and should not be considered part of this or any other report filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Our Annual Report on Form 10-K, Quarterly Reports on Form 10-Q and Current Reports on Form 8-K, as well as any amendments to those reports, are available free of charge through our website as soon as reasonably practicable after we file them with, or furnish them to, the SEC. The SEC maintains a website at www.sec.gov that contains reports, proxy statements and other information regarding SEC registrants, including Boeing.
Forward-Looking Statements
This report, as well as our annual report to shareholders, quarterly reports and other filings we make with the SEC, press and earnings releases and other written and oral communications, contain "forward-looking statements" within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. Words such as "may," "should," "expects," "intends," "projects," "plans," "believes," "estimates," "targets," "anticipates" and similar expressions generally identify these forward-looking statements. Examples of forward-looking statements include statements relating to our future financial condition and operating results, as well as any other statement that does not directly relate to any historical or current fact.

**Quantitative Report: Boeing (BA) as of 2024-07-01**

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
- **Revenue**: Q1 2024 revenue was $16.57B, down 7.5% YoY and 24.7% QoQ. TTM revenue (last 4 quarters) was $76.44B.
- **Earnings**: Q1 2024 net income was -$355M (EPS -$0.56). TTM net income was -$2.17B. The only positive EPS in the last 8 quarters was Q2 2022 ($0.32).
- **Margins**: Q1 2024 gross margin was 10.0% (per valuation ratios), operating margin -0.9%, net margin -2.8%. Margins have been volatile; e.g., Q3 2022 gross margin was -5.15%.
- **Cash-Flow**: No cash flow data provided for any period.
- **Balance Sheet**: Q1 2024 total assets $134.48B, total liabilities $151.50B, total equity -$17.02B. Total debt $47.69B. Debt has decreased from $57.01B in Q2 2022, but equity has remained negative since at least Q2 2022 (-$14.79B).

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
- **Earnings Quality**: Poor. Persistent losses (7 of last 8 quarters negative), high volatility in margins (e.g., gross margin range: -5.15% to 12.71%), and TTM operating income of -$710M. The QoQ EPS improvement (+1766.7%) is from a deeply negative base (-$2.70 in Q3 2023 to -$0.56 in Q1 2024) and does not indicate sustainable profitability.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience**: Weak. Negative equity of -$17.02B indicates liabilities exceed assets. Debt-to-equity is not meaningful due to negative equity. Debt reduction from $57.01B to $47.69B over 8 quarters is positive but insufficient to offset structural weakness.

**3. Valuation Versus Supplied History**
- **Latest Valuation (2024-06-28)**: P/S = 1.459, net margin = -2.8%, gross margin = 10.0%, operating margin = -0.9%. P/E, EV/EBIT, FCF yield, P/B, and ROE are unavailable.
- **Historical Net Margin**: Improved from -6.0% (2023-07-03) to -2.8% (2024-06-28), but remains negative. No historical P/S or other valuation multiples are provided for comparison.
- **Interpretation**: The P/S of 1.459 is not directly comparable to historical multiples due to lack of data. Given negative margins, traditional valuation metrics are not meaningful; the stock trades at a premium to sales despite ongoing losses.

**4. Price Level and Quarterly/Annual Return Context**
- **Price (2024-06-28)**: $182.01, within a 52-week range of $159.70–$267.54.
- **Returns**: Quarterly (from 2024-03-28) -5.7%; annual (from 2023-06-28) -13.6%.
- **Path**: Volatile quarterly moves: +34.1% (Q3 2023), -26.4% (Q4 2023), -5.7% (Q1 2024). The recent decline aligns with continued negative earnings and revenue contraction.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
- **Upside**: Debt reduction ($57.01B to $47.69B) and net margin improvement (-6.0% to -2.8%) suggest potential for operational improvement. If margins turn positive and revenue stabilizes, valuation could re-rate.
- **Downside**: Negative equity (-$17.02B), persistent losses (TTM net income -$2.17B), and high debt ($47.69B) risk further financial distress. Continued margin volatility (e.g., Q3 2022 gross margin -5.15%) could pressure the stock.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
- The financial trajectory shows persistent losses, negative equity, and high debt, with only marginal margin improvement. Valuation (P/S 1.459) is not cheap given negative earnings and lack of cash flow data. Price declines (-13.6% annual) reflect fundamental weakness. Quantitative risk is elevated due to balance-sheet fragility and earnings volatility; potential upside hinges on a turnaround not yet evidenced in the numbers.
BA.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Assessment: Boeing (BA) – As of 2024-07-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read (from 2023 10-K)**
Boeing is a major global aerospace company with four segments: Commercial Airplanes (BCA - 737, 767, 777, 787, 777X development), Defense, Space & Security (BDS - military aircraft, weapons, space systems), Global Services (BGS - aftermarket services), and Boeing Capital (BCC - financing). The business is heavily regulated, relies on a complex global supply chain, and faces strong competition from Airbus, Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and others in its various segments. The company highlights its extensive intellectual property portfolio and a workforce of approximately 156,000 employees (as of Dec 2022), including a significant unionized portion with key labor agreements expiring in 2024-2027.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated from Supplied Evidence)**
*   **Corporate Actions / M&A:** On 2024-06-25, an analyst discussed Boeing's reported proposition to acquire Spirit Aerosystems for approximately $35 per share, framing it as an expected strategic move.
*   **Contracts:** On 2024-06-27, Boeing won a $115 million deal to procure MH-47G helicopters for the U.S. Special Operations Command.
*   **Operational & Legal Concerns:** Multiple sources (2024-05-15 to 2024-06-24) report Boeing faces potential criminal prosecution for allegedly violating a 2021 settlement related to the 737 Max crashes. The Justice Department is deciding whether to pursue charges (2024-05-15). Concurrently, the FAA has demanded Boeing devise a plan to address "systemic" quality-control issues, with a deadline noted as running out at the end of May 2024 (2024-05-15).
*   **Financial Narrative:** Articles from 2024-06-28 highlight concerns over free cash flow following mixed quarterly results in April 2024 and note the stock is down 32% year-to-date in 2024.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risks:**
    *   **Legal & Regulatory Prosecution:** The imminent threat of criminal prosecution and/or a mandated federal monitor for manufacturing poses a severe risk to operations, reputation, and financials (2024-05-15, 2024-06-21, 2024-06-24).
    *   **Manufacturing & Quality Control:** The FAA's ultimatum to fix "systemic" quality issues indicates deep operational challenges requiring significant oversight and capital to resolve (2024-05-15).
    *   **Labor Relations:** Key union contracts (IAM) are set to expire in September 2024, introducing near-term risk of labor disruption (2023 10-K).
    *   **Competitive Pressure:** In commercial aviation, Airbus is an aggressive competitor, while in defense, Boeing faces entrenched players and new entrants like SpaceX (2023 10-K).
*   **Offsets:**
    *   **Long-Term Demand:** Industry trends favoring fuel-efficient aircraft and robust defense spending provide a long-term demand backdrop (Web context 2024-06-11, 2024-06-29).
    *   **Strategic In-sourcing:** The potential acquisition of Spirit Aerosystems (2024-06-25) could be a strategic move to improve supply chain control and quality over a key supplier.
    *   **Defense Backlog:** Ongoing contract wins in the defense sector (e.g., 2024-06-27 helicopter deal) provide a stable revenue base.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Negative.** The supplied evidence from the period immediately preceding the decision date is dominated by acute, material risks related to potential criminal prosecution, regulatory sanctions for systemic quality failures, and a depressed stock price reflecting severe operational headwinds. While the company has long-term strategic assets and end-market demand, the near-term qualitative narrative is overwhelmingly focused on legal, regulatory, and production crises that present substantial uncertainty and potential for value erosion. The potential Spirit Aerosystems deal is a strategic response but does not mitigate the immediate overhang of criminal liability and FAA intervention.

unsupported_claims: The qualitative report states Boeing 'reported proposition to acquire Spirit Aerosystems for approximately $35 per share' (2024-06-25). The raw evidence says the deal was 'reported' and 'approximately $35 per share' — the report omits 'reported' and adds 'approximately' without basis in the supplied text.

The qualitative report states the stock is down 32% year-to-date in 2024.' The raw evidence says the stock is down 32% year-to-date (as of 2024-06-28). The report's 'annual' return of -13.6% is measured from 2023-06-28 to 2024-06-28, which is a trailing 12-month return, not year-to-date. These are different metrics and should not be conflated.

Item 1.BUSINESS
General
Johnson & Johnson and its subsidiaries (the Company) have approximately 152,700 employees worldwide engaged in the research and development, manufacture and sale of a broad range of products in the healthcare field. Johnson & Johnson is a holding company, with operating companies conducting business in virtually all countries of the world. The Company's primary focus is products related to human health and well-being. Johnson & Johnson was incorporated in the State of New Jersey in 1887.
The Executive Committee of Johnson & Johnson is the principal management group responsible for the strategic operations and allocation of the resources of the Company. This Committee oversees and coordinates the activities of the Company's three business segments: Consumer Health, Pharmaceutical and MedTech (previously referred to as Medical Devices). Within the strategic parameters provided by the Committee, senior management groups at U.S. and international operating companies are each responsible for their own strategic plans and the day-to-day operations of those companies. Each subsidiary within the business segments is, with limited exceptions, managed by reside

 the risk of significant civil and criminal penalties. Of note is the increased enforcement activity by data protection authorities in various jurisdictions, particularly in the European Union, where significant fines have been levied on companies for data breaches, violations of privacy requirements, and unlawful cross-border data transfers.  In the U.S., the Federal Trade Commission has stepped up enforcement of data privacy with several significant settlements and there have been a material increase in class-action lawsuits linked to the collection and use of biometric data.
Further, the Company relies on global supply chains, and production and distribution processes, that are complex, are subject to increasing regulatory requirements, and may be faced with unexpected changes such as those resulting from the
4
COVID-19 pandemic and Brexit that may affect sourcing, supply and pricing of materials used in the Company's products.  These processes also are subject to complex and lengthy regulatory approvals.
Employees and Human Capital Management
As of January 1, 2023, and January 2, 2022, the number of employees were approximately:
|  | 2022 | 2021 |
| Employees1 | 155,800 | 144,300 |
| Full-time equivalent (FTE) positions2 | 152,700 | 141,700 |
1"Employee" is defined as an individual working full-time or part-time, excluding fixed term employees, interns and co-op employees. Employee data may not include full population from more recently acquired companies and individuals on long-term disability are excluded. Contingent workers, contractors and subcontractors are also excluded. Abiomed headcount has been included in the above table.
2FTE represents the total number of full-time equivalent positions and does not reflect the total number of individual employees as some work part-time.

Strategy
The Company believes that its employees are critical to its continued success and are an essential element of its long-term strategy. Management is responsible for ensuring that its policies and processes reflect and reinforce the Company's desired corporate culture, including policies and processes related to strategy, risk management, and ethics and compliance. The Company's human capital management strategy is built on three fundamental focus areas:
- Attracting and recruiting the best talent
- Developing and retaining talent
- Empowering and inspiring talent
Underpinning these focus areas are ongoing efforts to cultivate and foster a culture built on diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI), innovation, health, well-being and safety, where the Company's employees are encouraged to succeed both professionally and personally while helping the Company achieve its business goals.
Culture and Employee Engagement
At the Company, employees are guided by Our Credo which sets forth the Company's responsibilities to patients, consumers, customers, healthcare professionals, employees, communities and shareholders. Employees worldwide must adhere to the Company's Code of Business Conduct which sets basic requirements and serves as a foundation for the Company policies, procedures and guidelines, all of which provide additional guidance on expected employee behaviors in every market where it operates. The Company conducts global surveys that offer its employees the ability to provide feedback and valuable insight to help address potential human resources risks and identify opportunities to improve. In 2022, 92% of global employees across 77 countries participated in Our Credo Survey which was offered in 36 languages.
5
Growth and Development
To continue to lead in the changing healthcare landscape, it is crucial that the Company continue to attract and retain top talent. The Company believes that its employees must be equipped with the right knowledge and skills and be provided with opportunities to grow and develop in their careers. Accordingly, professional development programs and educational resources
are available to all employees. The Company's objective is to foster a learning culture that helps shape each person's unique career path while creating a robust pipeline of talent to deliver on the Company's long-term strategies. In furtherance of this objective, the Company deploys a global approach to ensure development is for everyone, regardless of where they are on their career journey. In 2022, 46.2% of employees in Manager and above job categories who had movements (including upward promotions or lateral transfers) took advantage of career opportunities by moving across functions, country or business segment lines (excluding employees in the research and development organizations). The Company's voluntary turnover rate was 9%.
Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI)
The Company is committed to workplace diversity and to cultivating, fostering, and advancing a culture of equity and inclusion. In 2022, Johnson & Johnson introduced the Company's evolved enterprise Diversity, Equity and Inclusion strategy, which recognizes how DEI accelerates the Company's ability to meet the changing needs of the communities the Company serves to deliver Our Purpose to profoundly change the trajectory of health for humanity. The Company's DEI vision is: Be yourself, change the world. The Company's DEI Mission is: Make diversity, equity and inclusion how we work everyday. Our evolved enterprise DEI Strategy is aligned to our DEI Vision and Mission and rests on four core pillars:
- Accelerate our global culture of inclusion where every individual belongs
- Build a workforce that reflects the diversity of our communities
- Transform talent and business processes to achieve equitable access and outcomes for all
- Drive innovation and growth with our business to serve diverse markets around the world
The Company's DEI strategy is guided by internal and external insights, global best practices and continual employee feedback which remind the Company that while diversity changes by location, inclusion is the same everywhere.
Compensation and Benefits
As part of the Company's total rewards philosophy, the Company offers competitive compensation and benefits to attract and retain top talent. The Company is committed to fairness and equitable treatment in its compensation and benefits for employees at all levels. The Company observes legal minimum wage provisions and exceeds them where possible. The Company's investment in employee health, well-being and safety is built on its conviction that advancing health for humanity starts with advancing the health of its employees. The Company has continuously expanded health and well-being programs throughout the Company and across the globe, incorporating new thinking and technologies to keep its offerings best-in-class and to help employees achieve their personal health goals. The programs and practices the Company advances for total health—physical, mental, emotional and financial—ensure employee health protection for emerging health risks. **Quantitative Report: JNJ (Decision Date: 2024-07-01)**

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
- **Revenue**: Stable near $21B quarterly, with Q1 2024 revenue of $21.38B. TTM revenue (last 4Q) is $85.65B. YoY revenue growth in Q1 2024 was +2.3%.
- **Earnings & Margins**: Volatile. Q1 2024 net income was $3.25B (EPS $1.34), down QoQ from Q4 2023's $4.05B ($1.67). A significant outlier is Q3 2023: net income $26.03B (EPS $10.21). TTM net income is $38.48B, inflated by this outlier. Gross margin is high at ~69.2% (latest). Operating margin declined to 23.4% in Q1 2024 from higher levels in prior quarters (e.g., Q2 2023: 29.3%).
- **Cash-Flow**: No operating or free cash flow data supplied for any period.
- **Balance Sheet**: Total assets decreased from $195.97B (Q1 2023) to $171.97B (Q1 2024). Total equity is stable near $70B. Cash decreased from $32.32B (Q1 2023) to $26.22B (Q1 2024). Total debt in Q4 2023 was $27.35B.

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
- **Earnings Quality**: Poor durability signaled. The TTM net income of $38.48B is dominated by a single quarter (Q3 2023: $26.03B). Excluding this quarter, trailing three-quarter net income is $12.46B. The YoY EPS change of -4566.7% reflects a low base (-$0.03 in Q1 2023) rather than stable growth. High gross margin (0.692) is offset by inconsistent operating income.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience**: Adequate liquidity with $26.22B cash versus $27.35B total debt (Q4 2023). However, total liabilities of $101.95B exceed total equity of $70.02B (debt-to-equity ratio ~1.45). The asset base has contracted, and equity is stable, not growing.

**3. Valuation Versus Own History**
- Current (2024-06-28) P/E diluted is **9.231**, at the very low end of its 365-day range of 9.1–22.3.
- P/B is **5.073**, at the low end of its 5.0–6.3 range.
- P/S is **4.147**.
- Earnings yield is **10.8%**.
- Historically, the P/E was consistently near 20x until early 2024, after which it compressed sharply to the current 9.2x. Net margin improved from 0.223 to 0.449 over the same period, largely due to the Q3 2023 earnings outlier inflating the trailing metric.

**4. Price Level and Quarterly/Annual Return Context**
- Price: $146.16 (2024-06-28), near its 365-day low of $143.13 (high: $175.97).
- Returns: **-7.6% quarterly** (from $158.19), **-10.3% annual** (from $162.96).
- Context: The price decline has occurred despite stable revenues. The valuation multiple has contracted significantly (P/E from ~20x to 9.2x), suggesting the market is pricing in concerns about the quality and sustainability of recent earnings.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
- **Upside**: Valuation is at a multi-year low (P/E 9.2, P/B 5.1). If earnings normalize to a level consistent with recent Q1 2024 profitability (~$3.25B quarterly, ~$13B annualized), the current low multiple could provide significant re-rating potential. High gross margins (0.692) and ROE (0.55, latest) indicate underlying profitability strength.
- **Downside**: Earnings are low-quality and non-recurring, as shown by the Q3 2023 outlier. The compressed multiple may correctly reflect this. Operating margins are declining. Cash generation is unquantifiable. Balance sheet leverage is meaningful. Price is already near 52-week lows, but further multiple compression or earnings disappointment could drive downside.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
- The fundamental picture is mixed: strong gross margins and a historically low valuation are offset by volatile, low-quality earnings and an incomplete view of cash generation. The severe price decline appears to reflect justified skepticism about earnings durability. The risk/reward is currently balanced between a potential value trap (if the low multiple is deserved due to poor quality) and a deep-value opportunity (if earnings stabilize near recent levels). A definitive conclusion is constrained by the absence of cash flow data and clarity on the recurring nature of operating profits.
JNJ.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Assessment Report: Johnson & Johnson (JNJ)**
**Decision Date: 2024-07-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read**
Based on the 2023-02-16 10-K filing, Johnson & Johnson is a global healthcare conglomerate organized into three segments: **Consumer Health** (personal care, OTC medicines), **Pharmaceutical** (immunology, oncology, neuroscience), and **MedTech** (medical devices, surgery, vision). The company announced in November 2021 its intention to separate the Consumer Health business into a new public company, Kenvue. The Pharmaceutical segment is the largest, with key products including STELARA and DARZALEX. The business is characterized by broad geographic reach, significant R&D investment, and a portfolio of established brands.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated)**
*   **Corporate Action / Legal Settlement (2024-06-11):** Johnson & Johnson reached a $700 million nationwide settlement with 42 state attorneys general over allegations that it promoted and misled consumers about the safety and purity of its talc powder products. The settlement requires the company to cease and not resume the manufacture and sale of the products.
*   **Market Narrative / Capital Allocation (2024-06-27 to 2024-06-30):** Multiple financial news articles (e.g., Benzinga, The Motley Fool) consistently highlighted JNJ as a top "dividend stock" or "dividend aristocrat" for income-seeking investors, emphasizing its high free cash flow and dividend payout. This narrative dominated the recent news flow.
*   **Financial Performance (2024-05-22):** A web snippet noted JNJ achieved revenue growth of 2.34% as of March 31, 2024, described as "remarkable performance."
*   **Competitive Landscape (2024-06-27):** News of FDA approval for AbbVie/Genmab's Epkinly in follicular lymphoma was noted, indicating ongoing competitive activity in the oncology space relevant to JNJ's pharmaceutical segment.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risk: Significant Legal and Reputational Overhang.** The 2024-06-11 talc settlement is a confirmed material event. The 10-K (2023-02-16) also references ongoing patent challenges and significant legal proceedings. The web context (2024-05-23) mentions allegations of "fraudulent" tactics related to prior bankruptcy strategies for talc liabilities.
*   **Risk: Key Product Patent Expirations.** The 10-K (2023-02-16) explicitly states that STELARA, accounting for ~10.2% of 2022 revenue, has a U.S. composition of matter patent that expired in 2023 and a European patent expiring in 2024. This presents a clear risk to future revenue.
*   **Risk: Regulatory and Pricing Pressure.** The 10-K (2023-02-16) details extensive risks from the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), which authorizes government price-setting for certain Medicare drugs starting in 2026/2028, and from global regulatory trends toward stricter enforcement and data privacy laws.
*   **Offset: Diversified Business Model.** The company's structure across Consumer Health, Pharma, and MedTech provides diversification. The 10-K notes raw materials are generally available from multiple sources.
*   **Offset: Perceived Financial Strength.** The recent dividend-focused narrative (June 2024) and the 10-K's mention of a triple-A credit rating (per web context 2024-06-02) suggest the market views the company as financially stable and capable of returning capital.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral.** The franchise is large, diversified, and operationally resilient, supported by a strong dividend narrative. However, the confirmed $700 million talc settlement (2024-06-11) and the imminent patent expiration for its largest product, STELARA (per 2023-02-16 10-K), represent material, near-term headwinds. The regulatory environment, particularly the IRA, introduces long-term uncertainty for the pharmaceutical segment. The balance of these confirmed risks against the company's scale and financial reputation results in a neutral qualitative outlook.

corrections.issue: The qualitative report states the 10-K is dated 2023-02-16 with period_end 2023-01-01, but the report's section on STELARA patent expiration says 'U.S. composition of matter patent that expired in 2023 and a European patent expiring in 2024.' The raw 10-K text confirms this language. However, the qualitative report does not note that the 10-K is 501 days old at the decision date and that a more recent 10-K (filing around early 2024 for fiscal year 2023) may exist but is not in the supplied data. The report does include a note about this gap, which is appropriate. No material issue.
corrections.required_action: No action needed; the age caveat is already noted in the raw evidence.

corrections.issue: The quantitative report states 'ROE (0.55, latest)' as an indicator of 'underlying profitability strength.' However, the raw evidence shows ROE jumped from 0.282 (through Jan 2024) to 0.511-0.55 starting Feb 2024, coinciding with the Q3 2023 outlier quarter rolling into the TTM calculation. The report does flag the Q3 2023 outlier elsewhere, but citing ROE 0.55 as a standalone strength signal without explicitly linking it to the same outlier that inflates TTM net income is inconsistent with the report's own earnings quality warning.
corrections.required_action: If using ROE as an upside argument, explicitly note it is inflated by the same Q3 2023 outlier that depresses P/E, and that normalized ROE is closer to the 0.28-0.30 range seen in the pre-outlier window.

corrections.issue: The qualitative report cites a Forbes article (2024-06-03) noting JNJ trades 'at a little under 14x' earnings, but the raw quantitative evidence shows P/E diluted at 9.231 as of 2024-06-28. The qualitative report does not reproduce this specific '14x' figure, so there is no direct contradiction in the specialist reports themselves. However, the Forbes snippet in the raw evidence could mislead if read alongside the quant report. No action required on the specialist reports.
corrections.required_action: No action needed; the specialist reports do not reproduce the Forbes 14x figure.

unsupported_claims: : The quantitative report states 'Cash generation is unquantifiable' — this is accurate per the raw evidence (all cash flow entries show 'no data'), so this is a supported claim, not unsupported. No unsupported claims identified.

The qualitative report's characterization of the $700M talc settlement as a 'confirmed material event' is directly supported by the raw evidence (2024-06-11 AG press releases). The STELARA patent expiration risk is directly quoted from the 10-K. The IRA regulatory risk is extensively documented in the 10-K text. All qualitative risk claims are supported.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: JNJ trades at a historically compressed P/E of 9.2x near its 52-week low, but this apparent value is largely an artifact of a Q3 2023 earnings outlier inflating trailing metrics; normalized earnings suggest a P/E closer to 14-15x, which is less compelling. The balance of material headwinds—$700M talc settlement, Stelara patent cliff, and IRA pricing pressure—offsets the valuation floor and dividend narrative, resulting in a neutral risk/reward.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: JNJ trades at a historically compressed P/E of 9.2x near its 52-week low, but this apparent value is largely an artifact of a Q3 2023 earnings outlier inflating trailing metrics; normalized earnings suggest a P/E closer to 14-15x, which is less compelling. The balance of material headwinds—$700M talc settlement, Stelara patent cliff, and IRA pricing pressure—offsets the valuation floor and dividend narrative, resulting in a neutral risk/reward.
key_factors: Valuation appears deeply compressed at P/E 9.2x (vs. 365-day range 9.1-22.3), but this is mechanically inflated by the Q3 2023 outlier quarter ($26.03B net income); normalized P/E on ~$13B annualized earnings is closer to 14-15x, which is fair rather than compelling.

Stelara patent cliff is confirmed and active: U.S. composition-of-matter patent expired in 2023, European patent expires in 2024, and Stelara represented ~10.2% of 2022 revenue per the 10-K, creating a measurable revenue headwind.

Talc litigation saw a $700M settlement with 42 state attorneys general on 2024-06-11, providing some resolution but the broader talc liability remains unresolved and the settlement itself is a cash drain.

Price at $146.16 is near its 365-day low of $143.13 with -10.3% annual return, reflecting justified market skepticism about earnings durability rather than creating a clear contrarian opportunity given the structural headwinds.

ITEM 1.  BUSINESS
In this report, the terms "The Coca-Cola Company," "Company," "we," "us" and "our" mean The Coca-Cola Company and all entities included in our consolidated financial statements.
General
The Coca-Cola Company is a total beverage company, and beverage products bearing our trademarks, sold in the United States since 1886, are now sold in more than 200 countries and territories. We own or license and market numerous beverage brands, which we group into the following categories: Trademark Coca-Cola; sparkling flavors; water, sports, coffee and tea; juice, value-added dairy and plant-based beverages; and emerging beverages. We own and market five of the world's top six nonalcoholic sparkling soft drink brands: Coca-Cola, Sprite, Fanta, Coca-Cola Zero Sugar and Diet Coke/Coca-Cola Light.
We make our branded beverage products available to consumers throughout the world through our network of independent bottling partners, distributors, wholesalers and retailers as well as our consolidated bottling and distribution operations. Beverages bearing trademarks owned by or licensed to the Company account for 2.2 billion of the estimated 64 billion servings of all beverages consumed worldwide every day.
We believe our success depends on our ability to connect with consumers by providing them with a wide variety of beverage options to meet their desires, needs and lifestyles. Our success further depends on the ability of our people to execute effectively, every day.
We are guided by our purpose, which is to refresh the world and make a difference. Our vision for growth has three connected pillars:
- Loved Brands. We craft meaningful brands and a choice of drinks that people love and enjoy and that refresh them in body and spirit.
- Done Sustainably. We grow our business in ways that achieve positive ch
…[truncated 20183 chars]…
djusted periodically by the Company, up to a maximum indexed ceiling price, and is adjusted quarterly based upon changes in certain sugar or sweetener prices, as applicable. The U.S. unit case volume prepared, packaged, sold and distributed under these legacy bottler's agreements is not material.
Under the terms of the bottler's agreements, bottlers in the United States generally are not authorized to manufacture fountain syrups. Rather, the Company manufactures and sells fountain syrups to authorized fountain wholesalers (including certain authorized bottlers) and some fountain retailers. These wholesalers in turn sell the syrups, or deliver them on our behalf, to restaurants and other retailers.
6
Promotional and Marketing Programs
In addition to conducting our own independent advertising and marketing activities, we may provide promotional and marketing support and/or funds to our bottlers. In most cases, we do this on a discretionary basis under the terms of commitment letters or agreements, even though we are not obligated to do so under the terms of the bottler's agreements between our Company and the bottlers. Also, on a discretionary basis in most cases, our Company may develop and introduce new products, packages and equipment to assist the bottlers. Likewise, in many instances, we provide promotional and marketing support and/or funds and/or dispensing equipment and repair services to fountain and bottle/can retailers, typically pursuant to marketing agreements. The aggregate amount provided by our Company to bottlers, resellers and other customers  of our Company's products, principally for participation in promotional and marketing programs, was $4.8 billion in 2022.
Investments in Bottling Operations
Most of our branded beverage products are prepared, packaged, distributed and sold by independent bottling partners. However, from time to time we acquire or take control of a bottling operation, often in underperforming markets where we believe we can use our resources and expertise to improve performance. Owning a bottling operation enables us to compensate for limited local resources; help focus the bottler's sales and marketing programs; assist in the development of the bottler's business and information systems; and establish an appropriate capital structure for the bottler. In line with our long-term bottling strategy, we may periodically consider options for divesting or reducing our ownership interest in a consolidated bottling operation, typically by selling all or a portion of our interest in the bottling operation to an independent bottler to improve Coca-Cola system efficiency. When we sell a consolidated bottling operation to an independent bottling partner in which we have an equity method investment, our Company continues to participate in the bottler's results of operations through our share of the equity method investee's earnings or losses.
In addition, from time to time we make equity investments representing noncontrolling interests in certain bottling operations with the intention of maximizing the strength and efficiency of the Coca-Cola system's production, marketing, sales and distribution capabilities around the world by providing expertise and resources to strengthen those businesses. These investments are intended to result in increases in unit case volume, net operating revenues and profits at the bottler level, which in turn generate increased sales for our Company's concentrate operations. When our equity investment provides us with the ability to exercise significant influence over the investee bottler's operating and financial policies, we account for the investment under the equity method.
Seasonality
Sales of our ready-to-drink beverages are somewhat seasonal, with the second and third calendar quarters historically accounting for the highest sales volumes. The volume of sales in the beverage business may be affected by weather conditions.
Competition
The commercial beverage industry is highly competitive and consists of numerous companies, ranging from small or emerging to very large and well established. These include companies that, like our Company, compete globally in multiple geographic areas, as well as businesses that are primarily regional or local in operation. Competitive products include numerous nonalcoholic sparkling soft drinks; water products, including flavored and enhanced waters; juices, juice drinks and nectars; dilutables (including syrups and powders); coffees; teas; energy drinks; sports drinks; milk and other dairy-based drinks; plant-based beverages; functional beverages, including vitamin-based products and relaxation beverages; and various other nonalcoholic beverages. These competitive products are sold to consumers in both ready-to-drink and non-ready-to-drink form. The Company has directly entered the alcohol beverage category in numerous markets outside the United States. In the United States, the Company has authorized alcohol-licensed third parties to use certain of our trademarks and related intellectual property on alcohol beverages that contain Company beverage bases. Competitive products include all flavored alcohol beverages containing various alcohol bases. In many of the countries in which we do business, PepsiCo, Inc. is a primary competitor. Other significant competitors include, but are not limited to, Nestlé S.A., Keurig Dr Pepper Inc., Danone S.A., Suntory Beverage & Food Limited, Unilever, AB InBev, Kirin Holdings, Heineken N.V., Diageo and Red Bull GmbH. We also compete against numerous regional and local companies and, increasingly, against smaller companies that are developing microbrands and selling them directly to consumers through e-commerce retailers and other e-commerce platforms. In addition, in some markets, we compete against retailers that have developed their own store or private-label beverage brands.
Competitive factors impacting our business include, but are not limited to, pricing, advertising, sales promotion programs,     in-store displays and point-of-sale marketing, digital marketing, product and ingredient innovation, increased efficiency in production techniques, the introduction of new packaging as well as new vending and dispensing equipment, contracting with marketing assets (theaters, sports arenas, universities, etc.), and brand and trademark development and protection.
Our competitive strengths include leading brands with high levels of consumer recognition and loyalty; a worldwide network of bottlers and distributors of Company products; sophisticated marketing capabilities; and a talented group of dedicated employees. Our competitive challenges include strong competitors in all geographic regions; in many countries, a concentrated
7
retail sector with powerful buyers able to freely choose among Company products, products of competitive beverage suppliers and individual retailers' own store or private-label beverage brands; new industry entrants; and dramatic shifts in consumer shopping methods and patterns due to a rapidly evolving digital landscape.
Raw Materials
We and our bottling partners use various ingredients in our business, including high fructose corn syrup ("HFCS"), sucrose, aspartame, acesulfame potassium, sucralose, saccharin, cyclamate, steviol glycosides, ascorbic acid, citric acid, phosphoric acid, caffeine and caramel color; other raw materials such as coffee, orange and other fruit juice and juice concentrates; packaging materials such as polyethylene terephthalate ("PET"), bio-based PET and recycled PET for bottles; and aluminum cans, glass bottles and other containers.
Water is a main ingredient in substantially all of our products. While historically we have not experienced significant water supply difficulties, water is a limited natural resource in many parts of the world, and our Company recognizes water availability, quality and sustainability, for both our ope
